reasoning: Although the 'Huaweigate' scandal has led to requests to lift the immunity of four active MEPs (Attard, Minchev, Martusciello, De Meo), they are highly unlikely to resign in the short term. As seen in the 2022 Qatargate scandal (e.g., Eva Kaili, Marc Tarabella), implicated MEPs strongly prefer to retain their seats; resigning would immediately strip them of their parliamentary immunity, expediting their potential arrest and prosecution, and cost them their salaries. Furthermore, the targeted MEPs have publicly denied wrongdoing and indicated their intent to defend themselves. The legal process in the Parliament to waive immunity typically takes months, meaning there is no immediate procedural mechanism forcing them out in June 2025. However, because the European Parliament has 720 members, there is always a baseline probability that at least one MEP might resign during any 21-day window for entirely unrelated reasons, such as accepting a national political appointment, entering local government, or dealing with personal/health issues. Accounting for both the unlikelihood of a scandal-driven resignation and the baseline rate of normal parliamentary turnover, the probability is set around 18%.
